State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662             322-5660              322-5901            322-6441              322-6444

April 30, 1982


Clayton P. Roche
Deputy Attorney General
Department of Justice
350 McAllister Street
San Francisco, CA 94102

Re: Your Opinion No. 82-314

Dear Mr. Roche:

Thank you for your letter requesting our views on the
above-referenced opinion which you are preparing. We start
out by stating that it is our conclusion that the "friends
committee" funds are either "contributions" or "gifts" within
the meaning of the Political Reform Act1/ (PRA). If they
are "contributions" and thus "campaign funds" within the
meaning of Elections Code Section 12401, then they
may not be used for personal purposes such as "to put a supervisor's
child through college or to enable the mayor to take a vacation."
If they are "gifts," then they may be used for whatever
purpose the recipient desires, but cannot be commingled with
the contributions. (Section 84307.) Gifts must be disclosed
as such under the PRA and any person or entity donating a
gift of $250 or more would then become a potential cause for
disqualification.

ANALYSIS

As pointed out in the correspondence from Mr. Burk E.
Delventhal, Deputy City Attorney of San Francisco, the Elections
Code provision dealing with "contributions" was repealed
this past year, by AB 1653, Chap. 160, Stats. 1981. The
Legislative Counsel's Digest to that legislation states as
follows:

LEGISLATIVE COUNSEL'S DIGEST

Under existing law, the Moscow Governmental Conflict
of Interests and Disclosure Act and the Waxman-Dymally

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014; unless otherwise
specified, all statutory references are to the Government
Code.

Clayton P. Roche
April 30, 1982
Page Two

Campaign Disclosure Act were superseded by the Political
Reform Act of 1974 on January 7, 1975. However, these
provisions currently contain language providing for the
limited and contingent operation of the respective
acts.

This bill would repeal both of these acts.

Chap. 160, Stats. 1981 p.1.

Thus, we turn to the PRA to find the meaning of "contri-
bution," which is defined in Section 82015 as follows:

"Contribution" means a payment, a forgiveness of a
loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforce-
able promise to make a payment except to the extent
that full and adequate consideration is received unless
it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it
is not made for political purposes. An expenditure
made at the behest of a candidate, committee or elected
officer is a contribution to the candidate, committee
or elected officer unless full and adequate consideration
is received for making the expenditure.

The term "contribution" includes the purchase of
tickets for events such as dinners, luncheons, rallies
and similar fundraising events; the candidate's own
money or property used on behalf of his or her candidacy;
the granting of discounts or rebates not extended to
public generally or the granting of discounts or
rebates by television and radio stations and newspapers
not extended on an equal basis to all candidates for
the same office; the payment of compensation by any
person for the same office; the payment of compensation
by any person for the personal expenses incurred on
behalf of a candidate or committee without payment of
full and adequate consideration.

The term "contribution" further includes any transfer
of anything of value received by a committee from another
committee, unless full and adequate consideration is
received.

* * *

Section 82015.

Clayton P. Roche
April 30, 1982
Page Three


     While the term "campaign funds" is not specifically
defined in either the PRA or the Elections Code, by process
of deduction from the provisions of the PRA relating to
campaign statements,2/ committees3/ and contributions,4/ and
the use of the term "campaign contributions" in Elections
Code Section 12400, it is clear that "campaign funds" consist
of "contributions." Therefore, the question of what is
covered by the prohibitions of Elections Code Sections 12400,
et seq. is determined by the definition of contributions
contained in Section 82015.

     For purposes of implementing the PRA the Fair Political
Practices Commission (Commission) has adopted regulations,
including 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215, which reads, where
pertinent, as follows:

     (a) A contribution is any monetary or nonmonetary
payment made for political purposes for which full and
adequate consideration is not made to the donor. A
payment is made for political purposes if it is:

          (1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting
to influence the action of the voters for or against
the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates,
or the qualification or passage of any measure; or

          (2) Received by or made at the behest of:

          (A) A candidate, unless it is clear from surrounding
circumstances that the payment was received or made at
his or her behest for personal purposes unrelated to
his or her candidacy or status as an office holder.
The term "payment" includes the candidate's own money
or property used on behalf of his or her candidacy;

          (B) A controlled committee;

                         * * *

_________________________________
2/  Sections 82006, and 84100-84307.

3/  Section 82013.

4/  Section 82015.

Clayton P. Roche
April 30, 1982
Page Four


     (b) "Made at the behest" means a payment made
under the control or at the direction of a candidate,
controlled committee, official committee of a political
party, or organization formed or existing primarily for
political purposes.

     Clearly, if "a supervisor sets up a friends committee
to solicit contributions ...," then this is a "controlled
committee" 5/ and payments "made at the behest" of the supervisor
to such a committee are contributions. Once received, they
become "campaign funds" subject to the prohibitions contained
in Elections Code Sections 12400, et seq., which state,
inter alia, as follows:

     12400. The Legislature finds and declares that
the political process is strengthened and public confidence
in elected officials will be enhanced by limiting uses
of campaign contributions.

* * *

     12401. Notwithstanding any other provision of
law, campaign funds, including funds of political action
committees, shall not be used for personal use.

     A payment from campaign funds is for personal use
if the payment creates a substantial personal benefit
and does not have more than a negligible political,
legislative, or governmental purpose....

     Clearly, payments "to put a supervisor's child through
college" and "to enable the mayor to take a vacation" do not
fall within the permissible nonpersonal purposes for which
contributions may be used. We also believe that, under the
express limitations of Elections Code Section 12403, if "the
mayor uses the money to purchase a car," then that is an
expenditure for personal use and is prohibited.

     We would reiterate that if friends of the mayor or the
supervisors wish to establish gift funds to which gift donations

_____________
5/ "Controlled committee" is defined in Section 82016,
to be a committee which is "controlled directly or indirectly
by a candidate.... A candidate controls a committee if he,
his agent or any other committee he controls has a significant
influence on the actions or decisions of the committee."

Clayton P. Roche
April 30, 1982
Page Five


are made for personal expenditures, then they may do so, but
disclosure and disqualification requirements would attach
thereto.  As to the other specific examples of uses of these
funds cited in the letters from Mr. Delventhal, we leave
them up to your analysis.

     Again, we thank you for permitting us this opportunity
for expressing our views on this question.  If you have any
questions, you may reach me at (916) 322-5901 (ATSS 8-493-
5901).

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert E. Deldigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:plh

350 McCAllister Street
San Francisco 94102
(415) 557-2544

State of California

Department of Justice

George Deukmejian
(Pronounced Duke-May-Gin)

Attorney General
(415) 557-1586

March 29, 1982

Mr. Ted Prim
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95801

Re: Opinion No. 82-314

Dear Mr. Prim:

Enclosed is an opinion request and attendant
materials we have received from the City Attorney of San
Francisco requesting our opinion concerning the applicability
of chapter 956, Statutes of 1981 to "friends committee funds."

Pursuant to our policy to request the views of
interested parties and associations, you are invited to submit
whatever views you may desire on the questions presented. Also,
if you do not wish to submit any views, please so advise us.

My administrative deadline for the preparation of a
response is May 7, 1982. Therefore, your prompt attention to
this letter will be appreciated.

Very truly yours,

GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN
Attorney General

Clayton P. Roche
Deputy Attorney General

CPR:lv
encls.

City and County of San Francisco:                                Office of City Attorney

George Agnost,
City Attorney

                                                                    March 3, 1992


Richard Martland
Office of the Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Martland:

     Stats. 1991, Ch. 956 added Chapter 5 (commencing with
Section 12400) to Division A (Election Campaigns) of the
Elections Code. The new law prohibits the expenditure of
"campaign funds", including expressly the funds of political
action committees, for personal use. We respectfully request the
office of the Attorney General, who is charged with the
enforcement of this Chapter, to render a legal opinion as to the
application of this prohibition to the funds of friends
committees. Are friends committee funds "campaign funds" within
the meaning of Chapter 5 and are friends committee funds thus not
to be expended for personal use, as defined by the 1992?

     Thank you for your attention to this request.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        GEORGE AGNOST
                                        City Attorney

                                        [signature]
                                        Burk D. Delventhal
                                        Deputy City Attorney


                                                            RECEIVED

                                                            MAR 2- 1992

                                                            OPINION UNIT


(415) 558 3315                    Room 234 City Hall                San Francisco 94102

City and County of San Francisco:                                   Office of City Attorney

                    George Agnest,
                    City Attorney


                                                                March 10, 1982


Mr. Richard Martland
Office of the Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, California 95814

Subject:  Our Request for Interpretation of Recent Amendments
          Adding to Division 9 of the Elections Code, Chapter V,
          Section 12400, et seq., Relating to Election Campaign
          Contributions

Dear Mr. Martland:

     Pursuant to our telephone conversation, I have enclosed
with this letter a copy of the relevant provision of our local
campaign contribution ordinance, i.e., Administrative Code
Section 16.503 (Definitions) and Section 16.508 which prohibits
contributions in excess of $750 to candidates or political
committees supporting or opposing candidates. We have always
interpreted this language to apply only to contributions for the
election or defeat of those persons seeking nomination or
election to office.

     Hence, "friends committees" have cropped up in San
Francisco and constitute a fairly significant loophole in our
local regulation of campaign contributions. For example, a
supervisor sets up a friends committee to solicit contributions
which the supervisor may use for expenses such as subscribing to
newspapers, hiring extra staff, or taking constituents out to
dinner to discuss problems that the legislator is dealing with.
Another example is the committee which solicits contributions
from friends of the mayor and the mayor uses the money to
purchase a car and to defray expenses incurred on trips in
connection with official duties.

     We have consistently concluded that the contributions of
these committees are not campaign contributions and are not
subject to the $750.00 limit. However, friends committees are
registered with the Fair Political Practices Commission. The
funds of these committees are reported on the same forms and in
the same manner as those of campaign committees.


                                                           RECEIVED

                                                      JUN 2 - 1982

                                                      OPINION UNIT

(W) 5953215                      F - 364 City H"                        San Francisco 94102

When recent amendments were adopted adding Chapter V to
Division 3 of the Elections Code, Supervisor John Nalbandian asked
our advice regarding the meaning of these additions. He asked no
specific questions, but we felt one important question was
whether the new limitations on the use of friends committee
funds for personal purposes. For example, would the new law
prevent the use of friends committee funds to put a supervisor's
child through college or to enable the mayor to take a vacation?

We note in Section 12400 reference to the importance of
enhancing public confidence by limiting "the uses of campaign
contributions". Then the next paragraph in
Section 12400 states that restrictions on the use of "political
funds" must be carefully drawn and narrowly construed to prevent
"the freedom of elected officials to carry out legitimate
governmental duties". Section 12401 states that "campaign funds"
including the funds of political action committee may not be
used for personal purposes. Section 12404 refers to the use of
"surplus campaign funds" if a candidate is defeated or when an
elected candidate leaves office. However these regulations in no
way define the terms used so that we can determine whether
contributions made to an elected official must be circumscribed
to legitimate governmental uses. While we feel that such an
application is within the intent of the new law and that such
limitation is necessary, we fear that the new law - like our code
- has a loophole.

The only definition we can find appears in Section 11516 of
the Elections Code. Our most recent version of the Elections
Code does not have that section in it. I don't know whether the
editors of the book erred, or whether Section 11516 was
repealed. In any case, Section 11516 defines a contribution to
mean any payment, gift, subscription, assessment, etc., "made
for the purpose of influencing the nomination or election of any
candidate . . . ."

If this definition applies in Chapter V, then Chapter V
cannot apply to friends committee contributions, especially
where a public official in making use of those monies can easily
avoids any "political campaign expenditures." Obviously if a
supervisor or a mayor has a nice car and has extra staff, the
political stature of that official may be enhanced, but those
expenditures really are expenditures for the public benefit, not
for the political welfare of the candidate.

It has been our opinion in the past that friends committees
and the contributions made thereto are not regulated by our local
ordinance. Further, we see no indication from the language in
the recent additions to the Elections Code, or from any other
provisions in the Elections Code, that would support a different
conclusion under state law.

It is for this purpose that we address our inquiry to you.

Very truly yours,

GEORGE AGNOST
City Attorney

Burk E. Delventhal
Deputy City Attorney

Elizabeth M. Katz
Legal Assistant

AMENDMENT OF THE WHOLE
PRESENTED IN COMMITTEE 7/15/80
As amended in Board 7/28/80

FILE NO.          131-80-2

ORDINANCE NO.     264/80

ADMINISTRATIVE
AMENDING SAN FRANCISCO CODE, SECTIONS 16.503, 16.508, 16.513,
16.514, 16.515 AND 16.516 REGARDING DEFINITIONS, CONTRIBUTION
LIMITATIONS, CITY ATTORNEY ADVICE, PENALTIES AND EFFECT OF
VIOLATION ON ELECTION.

NOTE: Additions or substitutions are
underlined; deletions are
indicated by [[double parentheses]].

Be it ordained by the People of the City and County of San
Francisco:

Section 1. San Francisco Administrative Code, Article XII,
is hereby amended by amending Section 16.503 thereof, to read as
follows:

Sec. 16.503, Definitions.

Whenever in this article the following words or phrases are
used, they shall mean:

(a) "Candidate" shall mean any individual listed on the
ballot for nomination for or election to any city and county
office, or who otherwise has taken affirmative action to seek
nomination or election to such office.

(b) "Charitable Organization" shall mean an entity exempt
from taxation pursuant to Title 16, Section 501 of the United
States Code.

(c) "Committee" shall mean any person acting, or any
combination of two or more persons acting jointly, in behalf of
or in opposition to a candidate or to the qualification for the

Page No. 1

ballot or adoption of one or more measures.

(d) "Contribution" shall be defined as set forth in the
Government Code of the State of California (commencing at Section
81000), provided, however, that "contribution" shall include
loans of any kind or nature.

(e) "Election" shall mean any primary, general or special
municipal election held in the City and County of San Francisco,
including an initiative, referendum or recall election.

(f) "Enforceant authority" shall mean the district
attorney of the City and County of San Francisco [or criminal
enforcement and the City Attorney for civil enforcement.    Nothing
in this article shall be construed as limiting the authority of
any law enforcement agency or prosecuting attorney to enforce the
provisions of this article under any circumstances where such law
enforcement agency or prosecuting attorney otherwise has lawful
authority to do so.

(g) "Measure" shall mean any city and county charter
amendment or other proposition submitted to a popular vote at an
election, whether by initiative, referendum or recall procedure
or otherwise, or circulated for purposes of submission to a
popular vote at any election, whether or not the proposition
qualifies for the ballot.

(h) "Person" shall mean any individual, partnership,
corporation, association, firm, committee, club or other
organization or group of persons, however organized.

Section 2. San Francisco Administrative Code, Article XII,

Page No. 2

Section 2. San Francisco Administrative Code, Article III,
is hereby amended by amending Section 16.508 thereof, to read as
follows:

Sec. 16.508. Campaign Contributions - Limitations.

(A) No person other than a candidate shall make, and no
campaign treasurer shall solicit or accept, any contribution
which will cause the total amount contributed by such person with
respect to a single election in support of or opposition to such
candidate, including contributions to political committees
supporting or opposing such candidate, to exceed five hundred
dollars ($500), provided, however, that for elections to be held
after January 1, 1981 the amount shall not exceed seven hundred
and fifty dollars ($750).

(B) (1) No person shall make, and no campaign treasurer
shall solicit or accept, any contribution which will cause the
total amount contributed by such person with respect to a single
election in support of or opposition to any measure requiring a
simple majority vote for its adoption to exceed five hundred
dollars ($500), provided, however, that for elections to be held
after January 1, 1981 the amount shall not exceed seven hundred
and fifty dollars ($750).

(2) No person shall make, and no campaign treasurer
shall solicit or accept, any contribution which will cause the
total amount contributed by such person with respect to a single
election in support of or opposition to any measure requiring a
two-thirds majority vote for its adoption to exceed one thousand
five hundred dollars ($1,500), provided, however, that for

Page No. 3

City and County of San Francisco:                    Office of City Attorney

                    George Agnest,
                    City Attorney


                                                             March 4, 1982


                    OPINION NO. 82 - 12

SUBJECT:           Use of Campaign Funds

REQUESTED BY:      Gilbert H. Boreman
                   Clerk of the Board of Supervisors

PREPARED BY:       Burk E. Delventhal
                   Deputy City Attorney
                   Elizabeth M. Katz
                   Legal Assistant

                    QUESTION PRESENTED

     What is the effect of new state legislation, requiring the
use of campaign funds, on local government office holders?

                         CONCLUSION

     See discussion below.

                        INTRODUCTION

     At the direction of Board of Supervisors President John L.
Molinari, you have brought to our attention Senate Bill 42. That
Bill, which appears as Stats. 1981, Ch. 956, took effect on
January 1, 1982. It adds Chapter 5 (commencing with Section
12400) to Division 9 (Election Campaigns) of the Elections Code.

     Prior to this addition, there was no state provision
regulating the use of campaign funds. However, Government Code
Section 3437 (added by State. 1979, Ch. 779) provides that "[n]o
contribution shall be commingled with the personal funds of the
recipient or any other person".

     You have requested "comprehensive comments as to the effect
[SB 42] will have on local government office holders".

     Chapter 1 (The Waxman-Dymally Campaign Disclosure Act) and
Chapter 2.5 of Division 9 expressly apply to chartered cities.
Elections Code Sections 11503 and 11802. Chapter 5 contains no
such provision. However, our Charter Section 9.103, paragraph 4,
provides in relevant part:


(WL) 50204
.

Gilbert U. Boreman                2                    March 4, 1982


     All provisions of the general laws of this
state, including penal laws, respecting the
registration of voters, initiative, referendum
and recall petitions, elections, canvass of
returns and all matters pertinent to any and all
of these, shall be applicable to the city and
county except as otherwise provided by this
charter or by ordinance adopted by the board of
supervisors as authorized by this charter
relative to any rights, powers or duties of the
city and county or its officers. . . . (Emphasis
added.)

     Since San Francisco has not otherwise provided for regulation of
the use of campaign funds, the new law applies to candidates and
office holders here.

                              ANALYSIS

     1.    The law prohibits the expenditure of campaign funds,
including the funds of political action committees, for personal
use.

     It appears to us that "campaign funds" within the meaning
of Chapter 5 may very well include the funds of friends
committees and thus that the law may prohibit the expenditure of
friends committee funds for personal use. We have addressed this
question to the office of the Attorney General and will forward
that office's response upon receipt. Until the Attorney General
rules on the question, we advise that no friends committee funds
be expended for personal use, as defined below.

     2.    The law specifies that an expenditure from campaign
funds is for personal use if the expenditure creates a
substantial personal benefit and does not have more than a
tangible political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
However, an expenditure from campaign funds is not for personal
use if it is to replace articles lost, damaged, or stolen in
connection with political, legislative, or governmental activity.

     3.    The law states that the following expenditures shall
be considered the personal use of campaign funds, and may not be
made unless there is a reasonable relationship to political,
legislative, or governmental purpose:

Gilbert H. Boreman                    3                    March 4, 1982


        a.  Payments for professional services or personal
debts, including, but not limited to, personal income taxes,
settlements of civil actions and related attorney's fees.
        b.  Payments or reimbursements for expenditures·'
incurred for travel.

        c.  Payments or reimbursements for personal gifts of
$100 or more.

        If a candidate's or office holder's expenditures for travel
have a reasonable relationship to political, legislative or
governmental purposes, payments or reimbursements for travel
expenses of the immediate family when traveling in conjunction
with the candidate or office holder shall be considered to be for
the same nonpersonal purposes.

        4.  The law prohibits expending campaign funds for
payments or reimbursements for the purchase, lease of more than
one year, or refurbishing of vehicles, appliances, equipment, or
real property where the lessor or owner is a candidate, office
holder, a member of his or her immediate family, or an officer of
a noncadidate controlled committee, or for the payment of
criminal fines, other than payments for traffic citations.

        5.  It specifies that surplus campaign funds under the
control of a former office holder or candidate or his or her
controlled committee may only be used or held for the following
purposes (upon either leaving elective office or at the end of
the post election reporting period):

        a.  The repayment of personal or committee loans or
other obligations having a reasonable relationship to political,
legislative or governmental activity.

        b.  The payment of outstanding campaign expenses.

        c.  Contributions to any candidate, committee, or
political party.

        d.  The pro rata repayment of contributors.

        e.  Donations to (1) a religious, scientific,
educational, social welfare, civic, or fraternal organization no
part of the net earnings of which inures to the benefit of any
private shareholder or individual, or (2) any charitable or
nonprofit organization which is exempt from state or federal
taxes.

Gilbert Y. Boreman                    4                    March 4, 1982


     f.  Held in a segregated fund for future political
campaigns not to be expended except for political activity
reasonably related to preparing for future candidacy to elective
office.

     6.  The law makes any person who willfully and knowingly
violates this act liable in a civil action brought by the civil
prosecutor for an amount up to $500 or twice the unlawful
expenditure, whichever is greater. If two or more persons are
responsible for any violation, they shall be jointly and
severally liable. In determining the amount of liability, a
court may take into account the presence or absence of good faith
and the seriousness of the violation.

     7.  It specifies that the Attorney General is the civil
prosecutor responsible for enforcing this act. The district
attorney of a county in which a violation occurs has concurrent
powers and responsibilities with the Attorney General. An
elected city attorney may act as the civil prosecutor concerning
violations occurring within the city pertaining to elections for
city office or city ballot measures.

     8.  The law permits any person to file with the civil
prosecutor a written request for the civil prosecutor to commence
an action. The request must specify the grounds for believing a
cause of action exists and must be signed under penalty of
perjury. The civil prosecutor must respond within 60 days.

     9.  It authorizes the court to award to a defendant who
prevails in such an action his or her costs of litigation,
including reasonable attorney's fees.

     10. The law specifies that the receipt, use, or
expenditure of any contribution shall not create an actual or
resulting trust, except as to provisions of law which make
persons entrusted with funds for the purpose of promoting or
defeating ballot measures liable for a fine of up to $5,000 and
imprisonment in state prison or county jail for specified periods
for misappropriation of funds.

     11. The law states that this act is not to be construed to
impose any new reporting obligations, nor to apply to the
expenditure of campaign funds in conjunction with any pending
litigation.

     12. It specifies that restrictions on the use of campaign
funds must be carefully drawn and narrowly construed to prevent
any chilling effect on the political process and the freedom of
elected officials to carry out legitimate governmental duties.

Gilbert H. Boreman                    5                    March 4, 1982


     13. At the same time, the law states that the political
process is strengthened and public confidence in elected
officials enhanced by limiting the use of campaign contributions.

     In answering your question, we have largely drawn on the
materials prepared for the state legislature's information in
considering this measure.

                                        Respectfully submitted,

                                        GEORGE AGNOST, CITY ATTORNEY

                                        Mark S. DeVenthal
                                        Deputy City Attorney

                                        Elizabeth M. Katz
                                        Legal Assistant
APPROVED:


GEORGE AGNOST

City Attorney
82603

Senate Bill No. 42


Passed the Senate September 15, 1981


Secretary of the Senate


Passed the Assembly September 15, 1981


Chief Clerk of the Assembly


This bill was received by the Governor this ________ 
day of __________________, 1981, at ______ o'clock ___M.


Private Secretary of the Governor


1 0

SB 42                          — 2 —

CHAPTER ______

An act to add Chapter 5 (commencing with Section
12400) to Division 9 of the Elections Code, relating to
campaign funds.

LEGISLATIVE COUNSEL'S DIGEST

SB 42, Carpenter. Campaign funds.
Under existing law, no provision is made for regulating
the use of campaign funds.
This bill would prohibit the personal use of campaign
funds, including funds of political action committees. This
bill would also restrict the disposition of surplus campaign
funds under the control of a former candidate or
officeholder or his or her controlled committee.
This bill would provide civil remedies for the violation
of the aforementioned provisions.
Article XIII B of the California Constitution and
Sections 2231 and 2234 of the Revenue and Taxation Code
require the state to reimburse local agencies and school
districts for certain costs mandated by the state. Other
provisions require the Department of Finance to review
statutes disclaiming these costs and provide, in certain
cases, for making claims to the State Board of Control for
reimbursement.
This bill would provide that no appropriation is made
by this act for the purpose of making reimbursement
pursuant to the constitutional mandate or Section 2231 or
2234, but would recognize that local agencies and school
districts may pursue their other available remedies to
seek reimbursement for these costs.

The people of the State of California do enact as follows:

SECTION 1. Chapter 5 (commencing with Section
12400) is added to Division 9 of the Elections Code, to
read:

(B 50

— 3 —                                                SB 42

CHAPTER 5.  LIMITATIONS ON USES OF CAMPAIGN
                FUNDS

12400.  The Legislature finds and declares that the
political process is strengthened and public confidence in
elected officials will be enhanced by limiting the uses of
campaign contributions. However, the making or receipt
of a campaign contribution does not create an actual or
resulting trust or a trust-like relationship limiting the uses
of campaign contributions.
  The Legislature further finds that any restrictions on
the use of political funds must be carefully drawn and
narrowly construed to prevent any chilling effect on the
political process and the freedom of elected officials to
carry out legitimate governmental duties.
  12401.  Notwithstanding any other provision of law,
campaign funds, including funds of political action
committees, shall not be used for personal use.  A
payment from campaign funds is for personal use if the
payment creates a substantial personal benefit and does
not have more than a negligible political, legislative, or
governmental purpose.  However, a payment from
campaign funds is not for personal use if it is to replace
articles lost, damaged, or stolen in connection with
political, legislative, or governmental activity.
  12402.  The following expenditures shall be
considered the personal use of campaign funds, and shall
not be made, unless there is a reasonable relationship to
political, legislative, or governmental purposes:
  (a)  Payments for professional services or personal
debts, including, but not limited to, personal income
taxes and settlements of civil actions, and related
attorneys fees.
  (b)  Payments or reimbursements for expenditures
incurred for travel.
  For the purposes of this section, payments or
reimbursements for travel by the immediate family of a
candidate or officeholder when traveling in conjunction
with the candidate or officeholder shall be considered to
be for the same purpose as the candidate's or
officeholder's travel.

                                                             91  69

sections.question: What is the effect of new state legislation, regulating the use of campaign funds, on local government office holders?
sections.conclusion:
See discussion below.

INTRODUCTION

At the direction of Board of Supervisors President John L. Molinari, you have brought to our attention Senate Bill 42. That Bill, which appears as Stats. 1981, Ch. 956, took effect on January 1, 1982. It adds Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 12400) to Division 9 (Election Campaigns) of the Elections Code.

Prior to this addition, there was no state provision regulating the use of campaign funds. However, Government Code Section 84307 (added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 779) provides that "[n]o contribution shall be commingled with the personal funds of the recipient or any other person".

You have requested "comprehensive comments as to the effect [SB 42] will have on local government office holders".

Chapter 1 (The Waxman-Dymally Campaign Disclosure Act) and Chapter 2.5 of Division 9 expressly apply to chartered cities. Elections Code Sections 11503 and 11802. Chapter 5 contains no such provision. However, our Charter Section 9.103, paragraph 4, provides in relevant part:

All provisions of the general laws of this state, including penal laws, respecting the registration of voters, initiative, referendum and recall petitions, elections, canvass of returns and all matters pertinent to any and all of these, shall be applicable to the city and county except as otherwise provided by this charter or by ordinance adopted by the board of supervisors as authorized by this charter relative to any rights, powers or duties of the city and county or its officers... (Emphasis added.)

Since San Francisco has not otherwise provided for regulation of the use of campaign funds, the new law applies to candidates and office holders here.

ANALYSIS

1. The law prohibits the expenditure of campaign funds, including the funds of political action committees, for personal use.

It appears to us that "campaign funds" within the meaning of Chapter 5 may very well include the funds of friends committees and thus that the law may prohibit the expenditure of friends committee funds for personal use. We have addressed this question to the office of the Attorney General and will forward that office's response upon receipt. Until the Attorney General rules on the question, we advise that no friends committee funds be expended for personal use, as defined below.

2. The law specifies that an expenditure from campaign funds is for personal use if the expenditure creates a substantial personal benefit and does not have more than a negligible political, legislative, or governmental purpose. However, an expenditure from campaign funds is not for personal use if it is to replace articles lost, damaged, or stolen in connection with political, legislative, or governmental activity.

3. The law states that the following expenditures shall be considered the personal use of campaign funds, and may not be made unless there is a reasonable relationship to political, legislative, or governmental purposes:

a. Payments for professional services or personal debts, including, but not limited to, personal income taxes, settlements of civil actions and related attorney's fees.

b. Payments or reimbursements for expenditures incurred for travel.

c. Payments or reimbursements for personal gifts of $100 or more.

If a candidate's or office holder's expenditures for travel have a reasonable relationship to political, legislative or governmental purposes, payments or reimbursements for travel expenses of the immediate family when traveling in conjunction with the candidate or office holder shall be considered to be for the same nonpersonal purposes.

4. The law prohibits expending campaign funds for payments or reimbursements for the purchase, lease of more than one year, or refurbishing of vehicles, appliances, equipment, or real property where the lessee or owner is a candidate, office holder, a member of his or her immediate family, or an officer of a non-candidate controlled committee, or for the payment of criminal fines, other than payments for traffic citations.

5. It specifies that surplus campaign funds under the control of a former office holder or candidate or his or her controlled committee may only be used or held for the following purposes (upon either leaving elective office or at the end of the post election reporting period):

a. The repayment of personal or committee loans or other obligations having a reasonable relationship to political, legislative or governmental activity.

b. The payment of outstanding campaign expenses.

c. Contributions to any candidate, committee, or political party.

d. The pro rata repayment of contributors.

e. Donations to (1) a religious, scientific, educational, social welfare, civic, or fraternal organization no part of the net earnings of which inures to the benefit of any private shareholder or individual, or (2) any charitable or nonprofit organization which is exempt from state or federal taxes.

f. Held in a segregated fund for future political campaigns not to be expended except for political activity reasonably related to preparing for future candidacy to elective office.

6. The law makes any person who willfully and knowingly violates this act liable in a civil action brought by the civil prosecutor for an amount up to $500 or twice the unlawful expenditure, whichever is greater. If two or more persons are responsible for any violation, they shall be jointly and severally liable. In determining the amount of liability, a court may take into account the presence or absence of good faith and the seriousness of the violation.

7. It specifies that the Attorney General is the civil prosecutor responsible for enforcing this act. The district attorney of a county in which a violation occurs has concurrent powers and responsibilities with the Attorney General. An elected city attorney may act as the civil prosecutor concerning violations occurring within the city pertaining to elections for city office or city ballot measures.

8. The law permits any person to file with the civil prosecutor a written request for the civil prosecutor to commence an action. The request must specify the grounds for believing a cause of action exists and must be signed under penalty of perjury. The civil prosecutor must respond within 60 days.

9. It authorizes the court to award to a defendant who prevails in such an action his or her costs of litigation, including reasonable attorney's fees.

10. The law specifies that the receipt, use, or expenditure of any contribution shall not create an actual or resulting trust, except as to provisions of law which make persons entrusted with funds for the purpose of promoting or defeating ballot measures liable for a fine of up to $5,000 and imprisonment in state prison or county jail for specified periods for misappropriation of funds.

11. The law states that this act is not to be construed to impose any new reporting obligations, nor to apply to the expenditure of campaign funds in conjunction with any pending litigation.

12. It specifies that restrictions on the use of campaign funds must be carefully drawn and narrowly construed to prevent any chilling effect on the political process and the freedom of elected officials to carry out legitimate governmental duties.

13. At the same time, the law states that the political process is strengthened and public confidence in elected officials enhanced by limiting the uses of campaign contributions.

In answering your question, we have largely drawn on the materials prepared for the state legislature's information in considering this measure.
sections.analysis:
As pointed out in the correspondence from Mr. Burk E. Delventhal, Deputy City Attorney of San Francisco, the Elections Code provision dealing with "contributions" was repealed this past year, by AB 1653, Chap. 160, Stats. 1981. The Legislative Counsel's Digest to that legislation states as follows:

LEGISLATIVE COUNSEL'S DIGEST

Under existing law, the Moscone Governmental Conflict of Interests and Disclosure Act and the Waxman-Dymally

; unless otherwise specified, all statutory references are to the Government Code.

Clayton P. Roche
April 30, 1982
Page Two

Campaign Disclosure Act were superseded by the Political Reform Act of 1974 on January 7, 1975. However, these provisions currently contain language providing for the limited and contingent operation of the respective acts.

This bill would repeal both of these acts.

Chap. 160, Stats. 1981 p.l.

Thus, we turn to the PRA to find the meaning of "contribution," which is defined in Section 82015 as follows:

"Contribution" means a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that full and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes. An expenditure made at the behest of a candidate, committee or elected officer is a contribution to the candidate, committee or elected officer unless full and adequate consideration is received for making the expenditure.

The term "contribution" includes the purchase of tickets for events such as dinners, luncheons, rallies and similar fundraising events; the candidate's own money or property used on behalf of his or her candidacy; the granting of discounts or rebates not extended to the public generally or the granting of discounts or rebates by television and radio stations and newspapers not extended on an equal basis to all candidates for the same office; the payment of compensation by any person for the same office; the payment of compensation by any person for the personal expenses incurred on behalf of a candidate or committee without payment of full and adequate consideration.

The term "contribution" further includes any transfer of anything of value received by a committee from another committee, unless full and adequate consideration is received.

embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 April 30, 1982 Clayton P. Roche Deputy Attorney General Department of Justice 350 McAllister Street San Francisco, CA 94102 Re: Your Opinion No. 82-314 Dear Mr. Roche: Thank you for your letter requesting our views on the above-referenced opinion which you are preparing. We start out by stating that it is our conclusion that the "friends committee" funds are either "contributions" or "gifts" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act1/ (PRA). If they are "contributions" and thus "campaign funds" within the meaning of Elections Code Section 12401, then they may not be used for personal purposes such as "to put a supervisor's child through college or to enable the mayor to take a vacation." If they are "gifts," then they may be used for whatever purpose the recipient desires, but cannot be commingled with the contributions. (Section 84307.) Gifts must be disclosed as such under the PRA and any person or entity donating a gift of $250 or more would then become a potential cause for disqualification. ANALYSIS As pointed out in the correspondence from Mr. Burk E. Delventhal, Deputy City Attorney of San Francisco, the Elections Code provision dealing with "contributions" was repealed this past year, by AB 1653, Chap. 160, Stats. 1981. The Legislative Counsel's Digest to that legislation states as follows: LEGISLATIVE COUNSEL'S DIGEST Under existing law, the Moscow Governmental Conflict of Interests and Disclosure Act and the Waxman-Dymally 1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014; unless otherwise specified, all statutory references are to the Government Code. Clayton P. Roche April 30, 1982 Page Two Campaign Disclosure Act were superseded by the Political Reform Act of 1974 on January 7, 1975. However, these provisions currently contain language providing for the limited and contingent operation of the respective acts. This bill would repeal both of these acts. Chap. 160, Stats. 1981 p.1. An expenditure made at the behest of a candidate, committee or elected officer is a contribution to the candidate, committee or elected officer unless full and adequate consideration is received for making the expenditure.
